text: ✅ STEP 1 — COMPLETE SQL TOPIC LIST (MASTER CURRICULUM)
This is a full structured syllabus (from beginner → advanced → industry level).You can use this as your eLab roadmap.
👉 (This is your MOST IMPORTANT module — you already started this)
🟢 MODULE 15: ADVANCED SYSTEM DESIGN (OPTIONAL FOR ELAB)
✅ HOW WE WILL BUILD THIS (VERY IMPORTANT)
For EACH topic, I will give:
SQL (Structured Query Language) is a declarative language used to interact with relational databases.
SQL is declarative, not procedural👉 You say WHAT you want, not HOW to get it
Q1. SQL is which type of language?A. ProceduralB. Object-OrientedC. DeclarativeD. Functional
Q2. SQL is mainly used for?A. Designing UIB. Managing databasesC. Writing OS codeD. Networking
A database is a structured storage system designed for:
RDBMS stores data in tables with relationships
Q1. RDBMS stands for?A. Real Data Base Management SystemB. Relational Database Management SystemC. Random Data SystemD. None
Q2. Which feature defines RDBMS?A. ImagesB. Tables with relationshipsC. FilesD. APIs
Syntax defines rules to construct SQL queries correctly
Q1. Which is correct syntax?A. SELECT FROM studentsB. SELECT * studentsC. SELECT * FROM studentsD. GET * FROM students
Q2. SQL executes SELECT first?A. YesB. No
Commands used to interact with database.
Q1. Which is DML?A. CREATEB. INSERTC. DROPD. ALTER
Q2. Which statement retrieves data?A. SELECTB. UPDATEC. DELETED. INSERT
CREATE TABLE employees (  id INT,  name VARCHAR(50),  salary DECIMAL(10,2),  join_date DATE);
Q1. Which data type stores text?A. INTB. VARCHARC. DATED. FLOAT
Q2. Which is best for salary?A. VARCHARB. INTC. DATED. TEXT
Table names can be case-sensitive in some DBs (Linux MySQL)
Q1. SQL keywords are?A. Case sensitiveB. Case insensitiveC. BothD. None
Q1. Best naming convention?A. studentDetailsB. student_detailsC. STUDENTDETAILSD. student details
CREATE TABLE is used to create a new table in a database.
👉 To store this → we need a table → created using CREATE TABLE
CREATE TABLE table_name (    column1 datatype,    column2 datatype,    column3 datatype);
CREATE TABLE students (    id INT,    name VARCHAR(50),    department VARCHAR(50));
👉 Table is now registered in database
CREATE TABLE employees (    id INT,    name VARCHAR(100),    salary DECIMAL(10,2),    join_date DATE);
CREATE TABLE students (    id INT PRIMARY KEY,    name VARCHAR(50));
CREATE TABLE orders (    order_id INT,    customer_id INT,    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(id));
CREATE TABLE enrollments (    student_id INT,    course_id INT,    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id));
CREATE TABLE employees (    id INT PRIMARY KEY,    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,    email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,    salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary > 0),    join_date DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE);
CREATE TABLE high_salary_employees ASSELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary > 50000;
CREATE TABLE products (    product_id INT PRIMARY KEY,    name VARCHAR(100),    price DECIMAL(10,2),    stock INT);
CREATE TABLE students (    student_id INT PRIMARY KEY,    name VARCHAR(50),    department VARCHAR(50),    marks INT CHECK (marks <= 100));
Q1. Difference between PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE?
Q2. What happens internally when table is created?
Q3. Can a table exist without primary key?
Q1. Which command creates a table?
A. INSERTB. CREATE TABLEC. ADD TABLED. NEW TABLE
A. NOT NULLB. UNIQUEC. DEFAULTD. CHECK
📘 MODULE 2 — SQL ALTER TABLE (MASTER ELAB GUIDE)
ALTER TABLE is used to modify an existing table structure.
CREATE TABLE students (    id INT,    name VARCHAR(50));
Q1. What happens to old rows when column is added?A. DeletedB. Filled with 0C. Filled with NULLD. Error
ALTER TABLE studentsADD (    age INT,    department VARCHAR(50));
Q2. What happens if datatype change is incompatible?A. Auto convertB. ErrorC. IgnoreD. Delete data
ALTER TABLE table_nameRENAME COLUMN old_name TO new_name;
ALTER TABLE studentsRENAME COLUMN name TO student_name;
Q3. DROP COLUMN will?A. Hide columnB. Delete column permanentlyC. Backup columnD. Rename column
ALTER TABLE ordersADD FOREIGN KEY (customer_id)REFERENCES customers(id);
ALTER TABLE ordersDROP FOREIGN KEY fk_name;
ALTER can be slow on large tables
ALTER TABLE studentsADD attendance INT DEFAULT 0;
Q1. Difference between ALTER and UPDATE?
Q3. Can we add NOT NULL column directly?
Q1. ALTER TABLE is used to?
A. Delete dataB. Modify structureC. Insert rowsD. Select data
A. INSERTB. ADD COLUMNC. UPDATED. CREATE COLUMN
A. DELETE COLUMNB. REMOVE COLUMNC. DROP COLUMND. CLEAR COLUMN
A. Only dataB. Only queriesC. StructureD. Index only
Add foreign key to orders table
Used for adding, deleting, modifying columns
📘 MODULE 2 — DROP TABLE vs TRUNCATE vs DELETE (MASTER ELAB GUIDE)
These 3 commands look similar but behave completely differently:
👉 This is one of the most asked SQL interview questions
DROP TABLE is used to completely remove a table from the database
Q1. DROP TABLE removes?A. Only rowsB. Only columnsC. Table + dataD. Only indexes
TRUNCATE removes all rows from a table but keeps structure
👉 Instead of deleting row by row:
Q2. TRUNCATE deletes?A. One rowB. Selected rowsC. All rowsD. Columns
DELETE removes specific rows from a table
Q3. DELETE can use?A. WHEREB. GROUP BYC. JOIN onlyD. None
👉 DELETE → row-level👉 TRUNCATE → page-level👉 DROP → structure-level
Q1. Difference between DELETE and TRUNCATE?
👉 Because it removes data pages directly
Q3. Can TRUNCATE be rolled back?
Q4. What happens when DROP is used?
Q1. Which command removes table permanently?
Remove all data from orders table
What happens if DELETE is used without WHERE?
INSERT is used to add new data (rows) into a table.
👉 It does NOT change structure👉 It only adds data
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2, column3)VALUES (value1, value2, value3);
👉 Order must match table structure exactly
INSERT INTO table_name (col1, col2)VALUES (value1, value2),(value3, value4);
CREATE TABLE users (    id INT,    name VARCHAR(50),    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'active');
INSERT INTO students (id, name, department)VALUES (5, 'Kiran', NULL);
NULL allowed only if column permits
INSERT INTO high_salary_employeesSELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary > 50000;
CREATE TABLE employees (    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,    name VARCHAR(50));
INSERT INTO daily_salesSELECT * FROM sales WHERE date = CURRENT_DATE;
Q1. Difference between INSERT and UPDATE?
👉 INSERT → new rows👉 UPDATE → modify existing rows
Q3. Can we insert multiple rows?
Q3. INSERT INTO SELECT is used for?
A. Delete dataB. Copy dataC. Modify dataD. Rename table
Q4. Auto increment is used for?
A. StringB. Unique ID generationC. SortingD. Filtering
Copy data from one table to another
UPDATE is used to modify existing data (rows) in a table.
👉 Teacher wants to update marks → use UPDATE
UPDATE table_nameSET column1 = value1, column2 = value2WHERE condition;
Q1. What happens without WHERE?A. ErrorB. No changeC. All rows updatedD. Only one row updated
UPDATE studentsSET name = 'Arun Kumar',    marks = 95WHERE id = 1;
UPDATE employeesSET salary = salary + 2000WHERE salary BETWEEN 30000 AND 50000;
UPDATE employeesSET salary = (    SELECT AVG(salary)    FROM employees)WHERE department = 'IT';
👉 Set salary of IT employees to average salary
UPDATE employees eJOIN departments dON e.dept_id = d.idSET e.salary = e.salary + 1000WHERE d.name = 'IT';
👉 Update data based on another table
UPDATE studentsSET grade = CASE    WHEN marks >= 90 THEN 'A'    WHEN marks >= 75 THEN 'B'    ELSE 'C'END;
UPDATE students SET marks = 90 WHERE id = 1;
Q1. Difference between UPDATE and DELETE?
Q2. Which clause is mandatory for safe update?
A. GROUP BYB. WHEREC. ORDER BYD. JOIN
UPDATE students SET name='Arun' WHERE id=1
Update salary of employees in IT department
Increase marks of all students by 5
DELETE is used to remove rows (data) from a table.
Q1. What happens if WHERE is not used?A. ErrorB. Only one row deletedC. All rows deletedD. No change
DELETE FROM employeesWHERE department = 'IT' AND salary < 40000;
DELETE FROM employeesWHERE salary BETWEEN 10000 AND 20000;
DELETE FROM employeesWHERE department_id IN (    SELECT id FROM departments WHERE name = 'HR');
👉 Deletes employees belonging to HR department
DELETE eFROM employees eJOIN departments dON e.dept_id = d.idWHERE d.name = 'HR';
👉 Delete data based on another table
👉 DELETE does NOT always immediately remove data👉 It may mark rows as deleted (depends on DB)
Indexes must be updated → slows deletion
DELETE FROM logsWHERE log_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL 30 DAY;
Q1. Difference between DELETE and TRUNCATE?
Q2. Can DELETE be rolled back?
📘 MODULE 4 — SQL SELECT (MASTER ELAB GUIDE — PART 1)
⚠️ This topic is HUGE (40% of SQL).So we will break it into multiple deep parts.
👉 This is Part 1 (Foundation + Core Concepts)Next parts will go even deeper (joins, analytics, internals).
SELECT is used to retrieve (read) data from a database.
SELECT salary * 12 AS annual_salaryFROM employeesWHERE annual_salary > 50000;
Functions on columns:👉 May disable index usage
SELECT name,CASE    WHEN marks >= 90 THEN 'A'    WHEN marks >= 75 THEN 'B'    ELSE 'C'END AS gradeFROM students;
A. Insert dataB. Delete dataC. Retrieve dataD. Modify table
A. SortingB. Removing duplicatesC. UpdatingD. Deleting
A. Best practiceB. Bad practiceC. RequiredD. Mandatory
📘 MODULE 4 — SQL FROM + WHERE (MASTER ELAB GUIDE — PART 2)
👉 This is where SQL becomes real analytics + filtering engine👉 Almost every query in companies depends on FROM + WHERE
FROM specifies where the data comes from
Q: What happens without JOIN condition?👉 Cartesian product (cross join)
SELECT *FROM (    SELECT id, marks FROM students) t;
Q: What is derived table?👉 Subquery inside FROM
WITH temp AS (    SELECT id, marks FROM students)SELECT * FROM temp;
WHERE filters rows based on conditions
SELECT * FROM studentsWHERE marks > 80 AND department = 'IT';
SELECT * FROM studentsWHERE department = 'IT' OR department = 'CSE';
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary BETWEEN 30000 AND 50000;
Why = NULL doesn’t work?👉 NULL is unknown value
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE department_id IN (    SELECT id FROM departments WHERE location = 'Chennai');
SELECT * FROM customers cWHERE EXISTS (    SELECT 1 FROM orders o    WHERE o.customer_id = c.id);
Q1. FROM clause is used for?
A. FilteringB. Selecting columnsC. Specifying tableD. Sorting
Q3. Which is correct for NULL?
A. = NULLB. == NULLC. IS NULLD. NULL =
Select employees in IT or HR
📘 MODULE 4 — SQL ORDER BY + LIMIT + DISTINCT (MASTER ELAB GUIDE — PART 3)
👉 This section is used in real analytics, dashboards, reports, ranking queries
ORDER BY is used to sort the result set
SELECT column_nameFROM table_nameORDER BY column_name [ASC | DESC];
SELECT name, marksFROM studentsORDER BY marks;
SELECT * FROM employeesORDER BY salary DESC;
SELECT name, department, salaryFROM employeesORDER BY department ASC, salary DESC;
SELECT name, salaryFROM employeesORDER BY 2 DESC;
👉 Bad practice in real projects👉 Breaks if column order changes
SELECT salary * 12 AS annual_salaryFROM employeesORDER BY annual_salary DESC;
SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY marks;
LIMIT restricts number of rows returned
SELECT * FROM studentsORDER BY marks DESCLIMIT 3;
SELECT department FROM students GROUP BY department;
SELECT DISTINCT departmentFROM employeesWHERE salary > 30000ORDER BY departmentLIMIT 5;
❌ Using ORDER BY without LIMIT in large data
SELECT * FROM studentsORDER BY marks DESCLIMIT 5;
SELECT * FROM productsORDER BY price DESC;
Q1. Difference between ORDER BY and GROUP BY?
👉 ORDER BY → sorting👉 GROUP BY → grouping
Q2. Why ORDER BY is slow?
Q3. How to get top 5 rows?
👉 DISTINCT removes duplicates👉 GROUP BY aggregates
Q1. ORDER BY is used for?
A. SortingB. FilteringC. Restrict rowsD. Joining
Use ORDER BY + LIMIT for top queries
📘 MODULE 4 — SQL SELECT (PART 4: FUNCTIONS + CASE + ADVANCED SELECT)
👉 This is VERY IMPORTANT for analytics, dashboards, and interviews
Functions are used to perform calculations on data
Aggregate functions operate on multiple rows → return single value
COUNT(*) counts?A. Only non-nullB. All rowsC. Only numbersD. Only strings
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total,AVG(salary) AS avg_salary,MAX(salary) AS highestFROM employees;
CASE is used for conditional logic inside SELECT
SELECT CASE    WHEN condition THEN result    ELSE resultENDFROM table;
SELECT name,CASE    WHEN marks >= 90 THEN 'A'    WHEN marks >= 75 THEN 'B'    ELSE 'C'END AS gradeFROM students;
CASE is used for?A. SortingB. FilteringC. Conditional logicD. Joining
SELECT name,CASE    WHEN marks >= 90 THEN 'A'    WHEN marks >= 75 THEN         CASE             WHEN attendance > 80 THEN 'B+'            ELSE 'B'        END    ELSE 'C'ENDFROM students;
SELECT name,(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) AS avg_salaryFROM employees;
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary > (    SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);
SELECT nameFROM employees eWHERE salary > (    SELECT AVG(salary)    FROM employees    WHERE department = e.department);
SELECT salary * 12 AS annualFROM employeesWHERE annual > 50000;
SELECT *FROM (    SELECT salary * 12 AS annual    FROM employees) tWHERE annual > 50000;
Q1. Difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column)?
👉 COUNT(*) → all rows👉 COUNT(column) → ignores NULL
Q3. Why alias not usable in WHERE?
A. JoiningB. Conditional logicC. SortingD. Grouping
A. JoinB. Scalar subqueryC. FunctionD. Index
👉 This module is the heart of WHERE clause filtering👉 Used in every real-world query
Logical operators are used to combine multiple conditions
SELECT * FROM studentsWHERE marks > 80 AND department = 'IT';
SELECT * FROM studentsWHERE department = 'IT' OR department = 'CSE';
Q1. AND returns rows when?A. Any condition trueB. Both trueC. None trueD. Only first true
Used to match multiple values in a single condition
Q2. IN is equivalent to?A. ANDB. ORC. JOIND. GROUP
Used to filter values within a range
SELECT *FROM tableWHERE column BETWEEN value1 AND value2;
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary BETWEEN 30000 AND 50000;
Q3. BETWEEN includes?A. Only startB. Only endC. Both start & endD. None
Index may not be used with % at start
Q4. '%' represents?A. One characterB. Any charactersC. Numbers onlyD. None
👉 NULL = unknown value👉 Cannot use = operator
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary IS NULL;
Q5. Which is correct?A. = NULLB. == NULLC. IS NULLD. NULL =
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE department IN ('IT', 'HR')AND salary BETWEEN 30000 AND 60000AND name LIKE 'A%';
SELECT * FROM studentsWHERE marks > 80 OR department = 'IT' AND city = 'Chennai';
SELECT * FROM studentsWHERE marks > 75 AND department = 'IT';
SELECT * FROM ordersWHERE amount BETWEEN 1000 AND 5000;
Q1. Difference between IN and OR?
A. One trueB. Both trueC. NoneD. Either
A. RangeB. Multiple valuesC. SortingD. Grouping
A. MathB. Pattern matchingC. JoiningD. Grouping
Find students in IT with marks > 80
👉 Functions are used to transform, calculate, and analyze data👉 This module is critical for analytics + GROUP BY + real-world SQL
Aggregate functions operate on multiple rows → return single value
COUNT(column) ignores?A. 0B. NULLC. Empty stringD. Negative
COALESCE returns?A. Last valueB. First valueC. First non-nullD. NULL
👉 COUNT(*) counts all👉 COUNT(column) ignores NULL
Q2. Which converts text to uppercase?
👉 This is the MOST IMPORTANT SQL TOPIC in real-world + interviews👉 I will cover:
Returns only matching rows from both tables
SELECT columnsFROM table1 t1INNER JOIN table2 t2ON t1.column = t2.column;
SELECT c.name, o.amountFROM customers cINNER JOIN orders oON c.id = o.customer_id;
SELECT *FROM employees eJOIN departments dON e.dept_id = d.idAND d.location = 'Chennai';
SELECT c.name, o.amount, p.product_nameFROM customers cJOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_idJOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.id;
INNER JOIN returns?A. All rowsB. Only matchesC. Only leftD. Only right
SELECT c.name, o.amountFROM customers cLEFT JOIN orders oON c.id = o.customer_id;
🔥 Example: Find customers with NO orders
SELECT c.nameFROM customers cLEFT JOIN orders oON c.id = o.customer_idWHERE o.customer_id IS NULL;
SELECT *FROM customers cRIGHT JOIN orders oON c.id = o.customer_id;
👉 Rarely used👉 Convert to LEFT JOIN instead
SELECT *FROM customers cFULL JOIN orders oON c.id = o.customer_id;
👉 Can crash queries with large data
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS managerFROM employees eLEFT JOIN employees mON e.manager_id = m.id;
SELECT *FROM customers cLEFT JOIN orders oON c.id = o.customer_idWHERE o.amount > 500;
LEFT JOIN orders oON c.id = o.customer_idAND o.amount > 500;
for each row in table1:    scan table2
SELECT c.name, SUM(o.amount)FROM customers cJOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_idGROUP BY c.name;
SELECT s.name, c.course_nameFROM students sJOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_idJOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.id;
SELECT city, COUNT(*)FROM customers cJOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_idGROUP BY city;
👉 INNER → only matches👉 LEFT → all left
Q4. How to find unmatched rows?
A. SortingB. Combining tablesC. DeletingD. Filtering
A. Only matchesB. All left rowsC. All right rowsD. None
A. ErrorB. Cross joinC. DeleteD. Sort
A. Joining same tableB. Joining 2 tablesC. SortingD. Grouping
📘 MODULE 8: GROUPING & AGGREGATION (ADVANCED ELAB MASTER VERSION)
SELECT dept, SUM(salary)FROM employeesWHERE salary > 30000GROUP BY dept;
SELECT dept, city, COUNT(*)FROM employeesGROUP BY dept, city;
SELECT dept, COUNT(*)FROM employeesGROUP BY deptHAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
SELECT dept, AVG(salary)FROM employeesWHERE salary > 30000GROUP BY deptHAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;
👉 HAVING without GROUP BY is allowed:
Sort data by GROUP BY column
SELECT dept FROM employees GROUP BY dept;
SELECT dept, SUM(salary)FROM employeesGROUP BY dept WITH ROLLUP;
GROUP BY dept, city WITH ROLLUP;
GROUP BY dept, city WITH CUBE;
SELECT dept, city, SUM(salary)FROM employeesGROUP BY GROUPING SETS (    (dept),    (city),    ());
SELECT dept, SUM(salary) totalFROM employeesGROUP BY deptORDER BY total DESCLIMIT 1;
SELECT dept, AVG(salary)FROM employeesGROUP BY deptHAVING AVG(salary) > (    SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);
SELECT dept,SUM(salary) * 100.0 / (SELECT SUM(salary) FROM employees) AS percentFROM employeesGROUP BY dept;
Q1. Why GROUP BY is slow?
Q4. Can HAVING be used without GROUP BY?
A. SELECTB. WHEREC. ORDER BYD. LIMIT
A. One rowB. All combinationsC. Only totalsD. Only rows
A. LoopB. Hash or SortC. JoinD. Index only
👉 ✅ Now truly ELAB LEVEL + INDUSTRY LEVEL + INTERVIEW MASTERED
👉 Subqueries = query inside another query👉 Used for:
A subquery that returns only one value (single row, single column)
SELECT column,       (SELECT single_value FROM table)FROM table;
SELECT name,       (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) AS avg_salaryFROM employees;
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary > (    SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);
👉 Must return only ONE value👉 Otherwise → ERROR
Scalar subquery returns?A. Multiple rowsB. Single valueC. Multiple columnsD. Table
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE department_id IN (    SELECT id FROM departments WHERE location = 'Chennai');
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary > ANY (    SELECT salary FROM employees WHERE department = 'HR');
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary > ALL (    SELECT salary FROM employees WHERE department = 'HR');
IN is used with?A. Single valueB. Multiple valuesC. SortingD. Grouping
Subquery that depends on outer query
SELECT nameFROM employees eWHERE salary > (    SELECT AVG(salary)    FROM employees    WHERE department = e.department);
👉 Employee salary > avg salary of their department
Correlated subquery runs?A. OnceB. Per rowC. NeverD. Random
SELECT name,       (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees) AS max_salaryFROM employees;
SELECT *FROM (    SELECT dept, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal    FROM employees    GROUP BY dept) tWHERE avg_sal > 50000;
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary > (    SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);
SELECT * FROM customersWHERE id IN (    SELECT customer_id FROM orders);
SELECT * FROM customers cWHERE EXISTS (    SELECT 1 FROM orders o    WHERE o.customer_id = c.id);
👉 EXISTS is better for large datasets
SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE department_id NOT IN (    SELECT id FROM departments);
👉 If subquery has NULL → returns NO rows
SELECT * FROM employees eWHERE NOT EXISTS (    SELECT 1 FROM departments d    WHERE d.id = e.department_id);
👉 Always prefer NOT EXISTS over NOT IN
SELECT e.*FROM employees eJOIN departments dON e.department_id = d.id;
SELECT * FROM ordersWHERE amount > (    SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders);
SELECT * FROM studentsWHERE marks > (    SELECT AVG(marks) FROM students);
SELECT dept, AVG(salary)FROM employeesGROUP BY deptHAVING AVG(salary) > (    SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);
❌ Using scalar subquery returning multiple rows
👉 Scalar → single value👉 Correlated → depends on outer query
A. TableB. Multiple rowsC. Single valueD. Index
A. ValueB. Row existenceC. SortingD. Join
Q3. NOT IN fails due to?
A. OnceB. Per rowC. NeverD. Random
Subqueries can be in SELECT, FROM, WHERE
👉 This is top-level SQL used in:
CASE is used for conditional logic inside SQL (like IF-ELSE)
SELECT CASE    WHEN condition THEN result    WHEN condition THEN result    ELSE resultENDFROM table;
SELECT name,CASE    WHEN marks >= 90 THEN 'A'    WHEN marks >= 75 THEN 'B'    ELSE 'C'END AS gradeFROM students;
SELECT customer_id,CASE    WHEN total_amount > 10000 THEN 'Premium'    WHEN total_amount > 5000 THEN 'Gold'    ELSE 'Regular'END AS customer_typeFROM customers;
SELECT name,CASE    WHEN marks >= 75 THEN        CASE             WHEN attendance > 80 THEN 'Eligible'            ELSE 'Not Eligible'        END    ELSE 'Fail'ENDFROM students;
👉 Avoid complex nested CASE on large data
Window functions perform calculations without collapsing rows
SELECT column,       FUNCTION() OVER (PARTITION BY column ORDER BY column)FROM table;
SELECT name,ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rankFROM employees;
SELECT name,RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rankFROM employees;
👉 Same rank for ties, skips numbers
SELECT name,DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC)FROM employees;
SELECT name, salary,LAG(salary) OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS prev_salaryFROM employees;
SELECT name, salary,LEAD(salary) OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS next_salaryFROM employees;
SELECT name, salary,SUM(salary) OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS running_totalFROM employees;
CTE = temporary result set defined using WITH
WITH temp AS (    SELECT ...)SELECT * FROM temp;
WITH avg_salary AS (    SELECT AVG(salary) AS avg_sal FROM employees)SELECT * FROM employeesWHERE salary > (SELECT avg_sal FROM avg_salary);
WITH cte1 AS (...),     cte2 AS (...)SELECT * FROM cte2;
Used for hierarchical or recursive data
WITH RECURSIVE cte_name AS (    -- Base query    SELECT ...    UNION ALL    -- Recursive query    SELECT ...    FROM cte_name)SELECT * FROM cte_name;

WITH RECURSIVE emp_tree AS (    SELECT id, name, manager_id    FROM employees    WHERE manager_id IS NULL    UNION ALL    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id    FROM employees e    JOIN emp_tree t ON e.manager_id = t.id)SELECT * FROM emp_tree;
May not always optimize (depends on DB)
SELECT product, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY sales DESC)FROM products;
SELECT date, SUM(sales) OVER (ORDER BY date)FROM sales;
SELECT name,DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY marks DESC)FROM students;
👉 GROUP BY reduces rows👉 WINDOW keeps rows
A. SortingB. Conditional logicC. JoiningD. Grouping
A. Remove rowsB. Keep rowsC. Delete rowsD. Insert rows
A. SameB. Skip vs no skipC. SortingD. None
A. Permanent tableB. Temporary resultC. IndexD. Join
👉 This is what separates beginners from professionals👉 Used in:
An index is a data structure that improves query speed
Instead of scanning whole book → jump to page
👉 DB scans all rows (Full Table Scan) ❌
CREATE INDEX idx_dept_salary ON employees(dept, salary);
Process of making queries run faster
Shows how database executes a query
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id = 10;
👉 Index works only if condition is SARGABLE
Filter data as early as possible
SELECT *FROM (    SELECT * FROM employees) tWHERE salary > 50000;
CREATE TABLE sales (    id INT,    year INT)PARTITION BY RANGE (year) (    PARTITION p1 VALUES LESS THAN (2023),    PARTITION p2 VALUES LESS THAN (2025));
A. DataB. Execution planC. TableD. Index only
A. SlowB. FastC. Index usableD. None
👉 This module connects SQL → Real Jobs (Data Analyst / Data Engineer)👉 Focus: cleaning, reporting, analytics, KPIs, ETL
SELECT name, COALESCE(salary, 0) AS salaryFROM employees;
WITH duplicates AS (    SELECT *,           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY id) AS rn    FROM customers)DELETE FROM duplicates WHERE rn > 1;
👉 Raw data from Excel → messy → SQL cleaning
SELECT MONTH(order_date) AS month,SUM(amount) AS total_salesFROM ordersGROUP BY MONTH(order_date);
SELECT department, AVG(salary)FROM employeesGROUP BY department;
SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS totalFROM ordersGROUP BY customer_idORDER BY total DESCLIMIT 5;
SELECT date,SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS running_totalFROM sales;
SELECT name,RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC)FROM employees;
SELECT date,AVG(amount) OVER (    ORDER BY date    ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)FROM sales;
SELECT customer_id,MIN(order_date) AS first_orderFROM ordersGROUP BY customer_id;
INSERT INTO clean_salesSELECT customer_id,SUM(amount) AS totalFROM raw_salesWHERE amount > 0GROUP BY customer_id;
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS users,SUM(amount) AS revenue,AVG(amount) AS avg_orderFROM orders;
SELECT product_id, SUM(amount)FROM ordersGROUP BY product_idORDER BY SUM(amount) DESC;
A. Extract Transform LoadB. Enter Transfer LoadC. Execute Table LogicD. None
A. Key Process IndicatorB. Key Performance IndicatorC. Key Product InfoD. None
A. JoiningB. Removing duplicatesC. SortingD. Indexing
A. GROUP BYB. WINDOW FUNCTIONC. JOIND. DELETE
👉 This module is VERY IMPORTANT for system design + interviews👉 It defines how data is structured, stored, and related
CREATE TABLE students (    id INT PRIMARY KEY,    name VARCHAR(50));
CREATE TABLE orders (    order_id INT,    customer_id INT,    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(id));
CREATE TABLE enrollments (    student_id INT,    course_id INT);
A. No duplicatesB. Atomic valuesC. IndexD. Join
A. Partial dependencyB. Transitive dependencyC. DuplicateD. Join
A. IndexB. Join tableC. DeleteD. Sort
👉 This module is CRITICAL for real-world systems (banking, payments, inventory)👉 Ensures data consistency, safety, and concurrency control
A transaction is a group of SQL operations executed as a single unit
BEGIN;UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2;COMMIT;
👉 Money transfer must be all or nothing
If one query fails → entire transaction fails
ACID ensures?A. SpeedB. SafetyC. SortingD. Joining
Creates a checkpoint inside a transaction
BEGIN;UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;SAVEPOINT sp1;UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2;ROLLBACK TO sp1;COMMIT;
👉 Keeps multiple versions of data👉 Readers don’t block writers
BEGIN;UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100;UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100;COMMIT;
📘 MODULE 15: ADVANCED SYSTEM DESIGN (OPTIONAL — BUT HIGH VALUE)
A Data Warehouse is a centralized system to store historical data for analysis
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM sales GROUP BY region;
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW sales_summary ASSELECT customer_id, SUM(amount)FROM ordersGROUP BY customer_id;
📘 MODULE 16: INTERVIEW + EDGE CASES (🔥 MOST IMPORTANT FOR JOB)
👉 This is what companies actually test
SELECT MAX(salary)FROM employeesWHERE salary < (    SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);
WITH cte AS (    SELECT *,    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY id) rn    FROM users)DELETE FROM cte WHERE rn > 1;
SELECT salaryFROM (    SELECT salary,    DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) rnk    FROM employees) tWHERE rnk = 3;
SELECT *FROM (    SELECT product_id, category,    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY sales DESC) rnk    FROM products) tWHERE rnk <= 5;
SELECT date, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)FROM activityGROUP BY date;
SELECT movie_id, COUNT(*)FROM watch_historyGROUP BY movie_idORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC;

A. TableB. Cached queryC. IndexD. Join
A. Ignore errorsB. Use EXPLAINC. Delete queryD. None
OLTP vs OLAP → transactional vs analytical
📘 MODULE 17: ADVANCED DATABASE FEATURES (FINAL ELAB EXTENSION 🚀)
🔥 87. SET OPERATIONS (UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, EXCEPT)
Set operations combine results of multiple queries.
SELECT column FROM table1UNIONSELECT column FROM table2;
SELECT city FROM customersUNIONSELECT city FROM suppliers;
SELECT city FROM customersUNION ALLSELECT city FROM suppliers;
SELECT city FROM customersINTERSECTSELECT city FROM suppliers;
👉 Returns rows in first query but not in second
SELECT city FROM customersEXCEPTSELECT city FROM suppliers;
Q1. Which removes duplicates?A. UNION ALLB. UNIONC. JOIND. GROUP BY✅ Answer: B
UNION → uniqueUNION ALL → fastINTERSECT → commonEXCEPT → difference
A virtual table based on a query
CREATE VIEW emp_view ASSELECT name, salary FROM employees;
View stores?A. DataB. QueryC. IndexD. Table✅ Answer: B
Precompiled SQL code stored in DB
CREATE PROCEDURE getEmployees()BEGIN    SELECT * FROM employees;END;
CREATE PROCEDURE getEmp(IN dept_name VARCHAR(50))BEGIN    SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = dept_name;END;
Stored procedure is?A. TableB. FunctionC. Precompiled SQLD. Index✅ Answer: C
CREATE TRIGGER before_insertBEFORE INSERT ON employeesFOR EACH ROWSET NEW.created_at = NOW();
CREATE TRIGGER log_deleteAFTER DELETE ON employeesFOR EACH ROWINSERT INTO logs VALUES (OLD.id);
Trigger runs?A. ManuallyB. AutomaticallyC. RandomlyD. Never✅ Answer: B
CREATE INDEX idx_emp ON employees(name, salary);
👉 Query uses only index → no table access
CREATE INDEX idx ON employees(dept, salary);
Best index usage?A. ScanB. SeekC. JoinD. Sort✅ Answer: B
SUM(salary) OVER (ORDER BY dateROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
JSON used for?A. NumbersB. Structured dataC. SortingD. Index✅ Answer: B
CREATE TEMP TABLE temp_sales ASSELECT * FROM sales;
PIVOT converts?A. Rows → ColumnsB. Columns → RowsC. JoinD. Sort✅ Answer: A
MERGE INTO target tUSING source sON t.id = s.idWHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATEWHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT;
BEGIN TRY   SELECT * FROM employees;END TRYBEGIN CATCH   PRINT 'Error';END CATCH;
GRANT SELECT ON employees TO user1;REVOKE SELECT ON employees FROM user1;
